message.content:
We built the sandbox package and implemented a darwin and linux runtime (vm creation implementation), that unfortunately has system dependencies like e2fsprogs or agent-fused, etc. We want a minimal implementation of the Runtime interface by using apple containers directly. We want to impement the following features: Agent Sandbox Security: Essential Features
This is a great topic, especially given where you are with Rune and the broader shift toward agentic tooling. Here's a comprehensive breakdown of what makes an agent sandbox effective at protecting the user.

Process Isolation
The foundation of any sandbox is strong process isolation. The agent's execution environment should be separated from the host system using one or more of these mechanisms:
Namespace isolation — The agent process should operate in its own PID, network, mount, user, and IPC namespaces. This prevents it from seeing or interacting with host processes, network interfaces, or filesystems it shouldn't touch. On Linux, this means leveraging clone() with appropriate CLONE_NEW* flags or using higher-level abstractions like containers.
Filesystem confinement — The agent should have a restricted view of the filesystem, ideally a chroot or overlay mount that exposes only what it needs. Writes should be captured in an ephemeral layer (copy-on-write) so the host filesystem is never mutated directly. Any changes can then be reviewed and selectively applied.
Seccomp and capability dropping — Even within a namespace, the syscall surface should be minimized. A seccomp-bpf profile that whitelists only necessary syscalls dramatically reduces the blast radius of any exploit. All Linux capabilities should be dropped except those explicitly needed.

Resource Controls
An agent that can exhaust CPU, memory, disk, or network is a denial-of-service vector against the user's own machine.
Cgroup limits — Hard ceilings on CPU time (both wall-clock and CPU-seconds), memory (with OOM kill rather than swap thrashing), PIDs (to prevent fork bombs), and I/O bandwidth. These should be non-negotiable defaults, not opt-in.
Execution timeouts — Every agent action should have a wall-clock timeout. If an agent hangs or enters an infinite loop, the sandbox should kill it deterministically. This needs to be layered: per-command timeout, per-task timeout, and a global session timeout.
Disk quotas — The ephemeral filesystem layer should have a hard size cap. An agent that fills a disk can destabilize the entire system.

Network Policy
Network access is one of the highest-risk capabilities you can give an agent.
Default-deny egress — The agent should have no network access by default. If network is needed, it should go through a proxy that enforces an explicit allowlist of domains/IPs and protocols.
No listening sockets — The agent should never be able to bind and listen on ports accessible from the host or LAN, preventing it from opening backdoors.
DNS filtering — Even with an allowlist, DNS queries should be intercepted and filtered to prevent DNS-based exfiltration or tunneling.
Rate limiting — Outbound connections should be rate-limited to prevent data exfiltration at scale even through allowed channels.

Permission and Capability Model
This is where most sandboxes fall short — they give binary access (sandbox on/off) instead of a granular capability model.
Declarative capability requests — Before execution, the agent should declare what it needs: filesystem paths (read/write/execute), network endpoints, environment variables, subprocess spawning, etc. The user reviews and approves a capability set before the agent runs.
Least privilege by default — If the agent doesn't request a capability, it doesn't get it. Unknown capabilities are denied, not granted.
Tiered approval — Low-risk actions (reading files in a project directory) can be auto-approved. Medium-risk actions (writing files, running build commands) get a one-time approval or session-scoped approval. High-risk actions (network access, running arbitrary binaries, modifying dotfiles) require explicit per-invocation approval.
Revocable permissions — The user should be able to revoke a granted permission mid-session. The sandbox must be able to enforce this immediately, not just on the next action.

Action Audit and Transparency
The user should never be in the dark about what the agent is doing.
Complete action log — Every syscall, file operation, network request, and subprocess invocation should be logged in a structured, queryable format. This is the agent's equivalent of an audit trail.
Real-time visibility — The user should be able to see a live feed of what the agent is doing, not just the final output. This includes commands being run, files being modified, and network requests being made.
Diff-based review — For filesystem mutations, the sandbox should present changes as diffs (like a git staging area) that the user can accept, reject, or modify before they're applied to the real filesystem. This is the single most important UX feature for trust.
Reproducibility — The action log should be detailed enough that the entire session can be replayed or audited after the fact.

Rollback and Recovery
Even with all protections, things go wrong. The sandbox must make recovery trivial.
Snapshot-based state management — The sandbox should snapshot the relevant state (filesystem, environment) before the agent begins. If anything goes wrong, one-click rollback to the pre-agent state.
Incremental checkpoints — For long-running agent tasks, periodic checkpoints allow partial rollback without losing all progress.
Transaction semantics — Ideally, agent actions are only committed to the real environment when explicitly approved. Until then, everything lives in a transactional overlay.

Secret and Credential Protection
Agents operating in a developer's environment are surrounded by sensitive material.
Environment scrubbing — The agent's environment should not inherit the user's shell environment by default. API keys, tokens, credentials in ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.config, etc. should be invisible to the agent unless explicitly passed through.
Credential injection, not exposure — If the agent needs to authenticate somewhere, credentials should be injected at the point of use (e.g., a proxy that adds auth headers) rather than exposed to the agent process. The agent never sees the raw secret.
Clipboard isolation — The agent should not be able to read the system clipboard, which often contains sensitive data.
Memory protection — After the sandbox session ends, all agent memory should be zeroed. No core dumps should be writable from within the sandbox.

Input/Output Sanitization
The boundary between the agent and the host is a trust boundary.
Output validation — Files produced by the agent should be scanned or validated before being applied. This includes checking for embedded scripts in documents, symlink attacks, or path traversal in generated filenames.
Prompt injection resistance — If the agent reads files or network responses as part of its context, the sandbox should flag or sanitize content that looks like prompt injection attempts. This is an emerging and genuinely hard problem, but at minimum the architecture should separate "data" from "instructions" as clearly as possible.
Symlink and hardlink restrictions — The agent should not be able to create symlinks or hardlinks that point outside its confined filesystem. This is a classic container escape vector.

Escape and Escalation Prevention
The sandbox itself must be hardened against breakout.
No ptrace — The agent should not be able to attach to other processes. PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS should be set.
Read-only root filesystem — The sandbox's own runtime (not the working overlay) should be immutable.
Minimal attack surface — The sandbox environment should contain the absolute minimum set of binaries and libraries. No compilers, debuggers, or network tools unless explicitly needed and approved.
Nested execution prevention — The agent should not be able to launch its own sandbox or container runtime, which could be used to escape the outer sandbox.. We only want to support darwin to start with.

The user wants to implement a minimal sandbox runtime using Apple's Containers framework directly, without external dependencies like e2fsprogs or agent-fused. They've provided a comprehensive list of security features they want to implement.

Let me first explore the existing sandbox package to understand:
1. The current Runtime interface
2. The existing darwin implementation
3. What Apple Containers APIs are available

Then I can plan a minimal implementation that uses Apple Containers directly.

Let me start by exploring the codebase structure.

    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package sandbox
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"io"
    20→	"net"
    21→)
    22→
    23→// VMState represents the state of a virtual machine.
    24→type VMState int
    25→
    26→const (
    27→	// VMStateUnknown indicates the VM state is unknown.
    28→	VMStateUnknown VMState = iota
    29→	// VMStateStarting indicates the VM is starting.
    30→	VMStateStarting
    31→	// VMStateRunning indicates the VM is running.
    32→	VMStateRunning
    33→	// VMStateStopping indicates the VM is stopping.
    34→	VMStateStopping
    35→	// VMStateStopped indicates the VM has stopped.
    36→	VMStateStopped
    37→)
    38→
    39→// String returns a string representation of the VM state.
    40→func (s VMState) String() string {
    41→	switch s {
    42→	case VMStateStarting:
    43→		return "starting"
    44→	case VMStateRunning:
    45→		return "running"
    46→	case VMStateStopping:
    47→		return "stopping"
    48→	case VMStateStopped:
    49→		return "stopped"
    50→	default:
    51→		return "unknown"
    52→	}
    53→}
    54→
    55→// Runtime is the interface for creating and managing sandbox VMs.
    56→type Runtime interface {
    57→	// Create creates a new VM with the given configuration.
    58→	// The VM is started and ready to accept connections.
    59→	Create(ctx context.Context, config *Config) (VM, error)
    60→}
    61→
    62→// VM represents a running sandbox virtual machine.
    63→type VM interface {
    64→	// ID returns a unique identifier for this VM.
    65→	ID() string
    66→
    67→	// State returns the current state of the VM.
    68→	State() VMState
    69→
    70→	// AgentConn returns a connection to the guest agent.
    71→	// The connection can be used to communicate with the agent
    72→	// running inside the VM via gRPC.
    73→	// Returns an error if the VM uses a different execution mechanism.
    74→	AgentConn() (net.Conn, error)
    75→
    76→	// Close stops and destroys the VM.
    77→	io.Closer
    78→}
    79→
    80→// ExecResult contains the result of executing a command in a VM.
    81→type ExecResult struct {
    82→	ExitCode int
    83→	Stdout   []byte
    84→	Stderr   []byte
    85→}
    86→
    87→// ExecOptions contains options for executing a command in a VM.
    88→type ExecOptions struct {
    89→	WorkingDir string
    90→	Env        map[string]string
    91→	User       string
    92→	Stdin      io.Reader
    93→	Stdout     io.Writer
    94→	Stderr     io.Writer
    95→}
    96→
    97→// ExecutableVM is an optional interface for VMs that support direct command
    98→// execution without going through an agent connection.
    99→type ExecutableVM interface {
   100→	VM
   101→	// Exec executes a command in the VM and returns the result.
   102→	Exec(ctx context.Context, command string, opts *ExecOptions) (*ExecResult, error)
   103→	// WriteFile writes a file to the VM's filesystem.
   104→	WriteFile(ctx context.Context, path string, content []byte, mode uint32) error
   105→	// ReadFile reads a file from the VM's filesystem.
   106→	ReadFile(ctx context.Context, path string) ([]byte, error)
   107→}
   108→

    15→package sandbox
    16→
    17→import "github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/sandbox/policy"
    18→
    19→// DefaultImage is the default container image used for sandboxes.
    20→const DefaultImage = "alpine:latest"
    21→
    22→// Config contains configuration for creating a sandbox executor.
    23→type Config struct {
    24→	// WorkspaceRoot is the path to the workspace directory to mount in the VM.
    25→	WorkspaceRoot string
    26→
    27→	// Policy contains security policies for the sandbox.
    28→	Policy *policy.Policy
    29→
    30→	// Resources contains resource limits for the sandbox VM.
    31→	Resources *ResourceConfig
    32→
    33→	// Image is the container image reference to use (e.g., "alpine:latest").
    34→	// If empty, DefaultImage will be used.
    35→	Image string
    36→}
    37→
    38→// ResourceConfig contains resource limits for a sandbox VM.
    39→type ResourceConfig struct {
    40→	// CPUs is the number of virtual CPUs to allocate.
    41→	// Default: 1
    42→	CPUs int
    43→
    44→	// MemoryMB is the amount of memory in megabytes to allocate.
    45→	// Default: 512
    46→	MemoryMB int
    47→
    48→	// DiskMB is the amount of disk space in megabytes to allocate.
    49→	// Default: 1024
    50→	DiskMB int
    51→}
    52→
    53→// DefaultConfig returns a Config with secure defaults.
    54→func DefaultConfig() Config {
    55→	return Config{
    56→		Policy:    policy.DefaultPolicy(),
    57→		Resources: DefaultResourceConfig(),
    58→	}
    59→}
    60→
    61→// DefaultResourceConfig returns a ResourceConfig with sensible defaults.
    62→func DefaultResourceConfig() *ResourceConfig {
    63→	return &ResourceConfig{
    64→		CPUs:     1,
    65→		MemoryMB: 512,
    66→		DiskMB:   1024,
    67→	}
    68→}
    69→

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//
     3→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     4→// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     5→// You may obtain a copy of the License at
     6→//
     7→//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package sandbox
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"fmt"
    19→	"os/exec"
    20→	"runtime"
    21→	"strings"
    22→)
    23→
    24→// DependencyError indicates a missing system dependency.
    25→type DependencyError struct {
    26→	Missing      []string
    27→	Instructions string
    28→}
    29→
    30→func (e *DependencyError) Error() string {
    31→	return fmt.Sprintf("missing dependencies: %s\n\n%s",
    32→		strings.Join(e.Missing, ", "), e.Instructions)
    33→}
    34→
    35→// CheckDependencies verifies that all required system dependencies are available.
    36→// Returns nil if all dependencies are satisfied, or a DependencyError with
    37→// installation instructions.
    38→func CheckDependencies() error {
    39→	var missing []string
    40→
    41→	// e2fsprogs tools - required for ext4 image manipulation
    42→	e2fsTools := []string{"mke2fs", "debugfs", "resize2fs"}
    43→	for _, tool := range e2fsTools {
    44→		if _, err := exec.LookPath(tool); err != nil {
    45→			// On Linux, mkfs.ext4 is an alias for mke2fs
    46→			if tool == "mke2fs" {
    47→				if _, err := exec.LookPath("mkfs.ext4"); err != nil {
    48→					missing = append(missing, tool)
    49→				}
    50→			} else {
    51→				missing = append(missing, tool)
    52→			}
    53→		}
    54→	}
    55→
    56→	if len(missing) == 0 {
    57→		return nil
    58→	}
    59→
    60→	var instructions string
    61→	switch runtime.GOOS {
    62→	case "darwin":
    63→		instructions = `Install e2fsprogs using Homebrew:
    64→
    65→    brew install e2fsprogs
    66→
    67→After installation, ensure the tools are in your PATH. Homebrew may install
    68→them as keg-only, so you might need to add them to your PATH:
    69→
    70→    export PATH="/usr/local/opt/e2fsprogs/bin:$PATH"  # Intel Mac
    71→    export PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/e2fsprogs/bin:$PATH"  # Apple Silicon
    72→
    73→You can add this to your shell profile (~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc).`
    74→
    75→	case "linux":
    76→		instructions = `Install e2fsprogs using your package manager:
    77→
    78→    # Debian/Ubuntu
    79→    sudo apt-get install e2fsprogs
    80→
    81→    # Fedora/RHEL/CentOS
    82→    sudo dnf install e2fsprogs
    83→
    84→    # Arch Linux
    85→    sudo pacman -S e2fsprogs`
    86→
    87→	default:
    88→		instructions = "Install e2fsprogs for your operating system."
    89→	}
    90→
    91→	return &DependencyError{
    92→		Missing:      missing,
    93→		Instructions: instructions,
    94→	}
    95→}
    96→
    97→// checkE2fsprogs is a quick check for e2fsprogs availability.
    98→// Returns an error with installation instructions if not found.
    99→func checkE2fsprogs() error {
   100→	// Check for mke2fs (or mkfs.ext4 on Linux)
   101→	if _, err := exec.LookPath("mke2fs"); err != nil {
   102→		if runtime.GOOS == "linux" {
   103→			if _, err := exec.LookPath("mkfs.ext4"); err == nil {
   104→				return nil
   105→			}
   106→		}
   107→		return CheckDependencies()
   108→	}
   109→	return nil
   110→}

    19→// ErrInvalidCommand is returned when a command is invalid (e.g., empty path).
    20→var ErrInvalidCommand = errors.New("sandbox: invalid command")
    21→
    22→// ErrProcessNotFound is returned when a process cannot be found.
    23→var ErrProcessNotFound = errors.New("sandbox: process not found")
    24→
    25→// ErrVMNotRunning is returned when the VM is not running.
    26→var ErrVMNotRunning = errors.New("sandbox: vm not running")
    27→
    28→// ErrAgentNotConnected is returned when the guest agent is not connected.
    29→var ErrAgentNotConnected = errors.New("sandbox: agent not connected")
    30→
    31→// ErrUnsupportedPlatform is returned when the platform is not supported.
    32→var ErrUnsupportedPlatform = errors.New("sandbox: unsupported platform")
    33→
    34→// ErrTimeout is returned when an operation times out.
    35→var ErrTimeout = errors.New("sandbox: timeout")
    36→
    37→// ErrClosed is returned when the executor has been closed.
    38→var ErrClosed = errors.New("sandbox: executor closed")

content.content:
1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//
     3→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     4→// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     5→// You may obtain a copy of the License at
     6→//
     7→//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package sandbox
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"fmt"
    20→	"io"
    21→	"net/http"
    22→	"os"
    23→	"path/filepath"
    24→	"runtime"
    25→)
    26→
    27→const (
    28→	// matchlockVersion is the version of Matchlock guest binaries to download.
    29→	// This should match the version in go.mod.
    30→	matchlockVersion = "v0.1.11"
    31→
    32→	// githubReleaseURL is the base URL for Matchlock GitHub releases.
    33→	githubReleaseURL = "https://github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock/releases/download"
    34→)
    35→
    36→// guestBinaryManager handles downloading and caching guest binaries.
    37→type guestBinaryManager struct {
    38→	cacheDir string
    39→	version  string
    40→}
    41→
    42→// newGuestBinaryManager creates a new guest binary manager.
    43→func newGuestBinaryManager() *guestBinaryManager {
    44→	home, _ := os.UserHomeDir()
    45→	return &guestBinaryManager{
    46→		cacheDir: filepath.Join(home, ".cache", "matchlock"),
    47→		version:  matchlockVersion,
    48→	}
    49→}
    50→
    51→// guestArch returns the architecture suffix for guest binaries.
    52→// Guest binaries are always Linux binaries (they run inside the VM).
    53→func guestArch() string {
    54→	// On macOS arm64, the VM runs Linux arm64
    55→	// On macOS x86_64, the VM runs Linux x86_64 (but we don't support this)
    56→	// On Linux, match the host architecture
    57→	if runtime.GOARCH == "arm64" {
    58→		return "linux-arm64"
    59→	}
    60→	return "linux-amd64"
    61→}
    62→
    63→// EnsureGuestBinaries downloads guest-agent and guest-fused if not present.
    64→// Returns the paths to the binaries.
    65→func (m *guestBinaryManager) EnsureGuestBinaries(ctx context.Context) (agentPath, fusedPath string, err error) {
    66→	arch := guestArch()
    67→
    68→	agentPath = filepath.Join(m.cacheDir, "guest-agent")
    69→	fusedPath = filepath.Join(m.cacheDir, "guest-fused")
    70→
    71→	// Check if binaries already exist and are valid
    72→	if m.isValidBinary(agentPath, arch) && m.isValidBinary(fusedPath, arch) {
    73→		return agentPath, fusedPath, nil
    74→	}
    75→
    76→	// Create cache directory
    77→	if err := os.MkdirAll(m.cacheDir, 0755); err != nil {
    78→		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("create cache dir: %w", err)
    79→	}
    80→
    81→	// Download guest-agent
    82→	agentURL := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s/guest-agent-%s", githubReleaseURL, m.version, arch)
    83→	if err := m.downloadBinary(ctx, agentURL, agentPath); err != nil {
    84→		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("download guest-agent: %w", err)
    85→	}
    86→
    87→	// Download guest-fused
    88→	fusedURL := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s/guest-fused-%s", githubReleaseURL, m.version, arch)
    89→	if err := m.downloadBinary(ctx, fusedURL, fusedPath); err != nil {
    90→		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("download guest-fused: %w", err)
    91→	}
    92→
    93→	return agentPath, fusedPath, nil
    94→}
    95→
    96→// isValidBinary checks if a binary exists and has the expected architecture.
    97→func (m *guestBinaryManager) isValidBinary(path, expectedArch string) bool {
    98→	fi, err := os.Stat(path)
    99→	if err != nil || fi.Size() == 0 {
   100→		return false
   101→	}
   102→
   103→	// Check ELF header for architecture
   104→	f, err := os.Open(path)
   105→	if err != nil {
   106→		return false
   107→	}
   108→	defer f.Close()
   109→
   110→	// Read ELF magic
   111→	magic := make([]byte, 4)
   112→	if _, err := f.Read(magic); err != nil {
   113→		return false
   114→	}
   115→	if magic[0] != 0x7f || magic[1] != 'E' || magic[2] != 'L' || magic[3] != 'F' {
   116→		return false
   117→	}
   118→
   119→	// Read e_machine at offset 18
   120→	if _, err := f.Seek(18, 0); err != nil {
   121→		return false
   122→	}
   123→	machineBytes := make([]byte, 2)
   124→	if _, err := f.Read(machineBytes); err != nil {
   125→		return false
   126→	}
   127→	machine := uint16(machineBytes[0]) | uint16(machineBytes[1])<<8
   128→
   129→	// EM_AARCH64=183, EM_X86_64=62
   130→	switch expectedArch {
   131→	case "linux-arm64":
   132→		return machine == 183
   133→	case "linux-amd64":
   134→		return machine == 62
   135→	}
   136→	return true
   137→}
   138→
   139→// downloadBinary downloads a file from URL to destPath.
   140→func (m *guestBinaryManager) downloadBinary(ctx context.Context, url, destPath string) error {
   141→	req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, url, nil)
   142→	if err != nil {
   143→		return fmt.Errorf("create request: %w", err)
   144→	}
   145→
   146→	resp, err := http.DefaultClient.Do(req)
   147→	if err != nil {
   148→		return fmt.Errorf("download: %w", err)
   149→	}
   150→	defer resp.Body.Close()
   151→
   152→	if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
   153→		return fmt.Errorf("download failed: HTTP %d", resp.StatusCode)
   154→	}
   155→
   156→	// Write to temp file first
   157→	tmpPath := destPath + ".tmp"
   158→	f, err := os.OpenFile(tmpPath, os.O_CREATE|os.O_WRONLY|os.O_TRUNC, 0755)
   159→	if err != nil {
   160→		return fmt.Errorf("create temp file: %w", err)
   161→	}
   162→
   163→	_, err = io.Copy(f, resp.Body)
   164→	if closeErr := f.Close(); closeErr != nil && err == nil {
   165→		err = closeErr
   166→	}
   167→	if err != nil {
   168→		os.Remove(tmpPath)
   169→		return fmt.Errorf("write file: %w", err)
   170→	}
   171→
   172→	// Atomic rename
   173→	if err := os.Rename(tmpPath, destPath); err != nil {
   174→		os.Remove(tmpPath)
   175→		return fmt.Errorf("rename: %w", err)
   176→	}
   177→
   178→	return nil
   179→}
   180→
   181→// ensureGuestBinaries is a convenience function to ensure guest binaries are downloaded.
   182→func ensureGuestBinaries(ctx context.Context) error {
   183→	mgr := newGuestBinaryManager()
   184→	_, _, err := mgr.EnsureGuestBinaries(ctx)
   185→	return err
   186→}
   187→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→// Package policy provides security policy definitions for sandbox execution.
    16→package policy
    17→
    18→// Policy combines all security constraints for a sandbox.
    19→type Policy struct {
    20→	// Network contains network access policies.
    21→	Network *NetworkPolicy
    22→
    23→	// Filesystem contains filesystem access policies.
    24→	Filesystem *FilesystemPolicy
    25→
    26→	// Environment contains environment variable policies.
    27→	Environment *EnvironmentPolicy
    28→
    29→	// Audit contains audit logging configuration.
    30→	Audit *AuditPolicy
    31→}
    32→
    33→// AuditPolicy contains configuration for audit logging.
    34→type AuditPolicy struct {
    35→	// LogCommands enables logging of all commands executed.
    36→	LogCommands bool
    37→
    38→	// LogNetwork enables logging of network connections.
    39→	LogNetwork bool
    40→
    41→	// LogFilesystem enables logging of filesystem operations.
    42→	LogFilesystem bool
    43→}
    44→
    45→// DefaultPolicy returns a Policy with secure defaults.
    46→func DefaultPolicy() *Policy {
    47→	return &Policy{
    48→		Network:     DefaultNetworkPolicy(),
    49→		Filesystem:  DefaultFilesystemPolicy(),
    50→		Environment: DefaultEnvironmentPolicy(),
    51→		Audit:       DefaultAuditPolicy(),
    52→	}
    53→}
    54→
    55→// DefaultAuditPolicy returns an AuditPolicy with defaults.
    56→func DefaultAuditPolicy() *AuditPolicy {
    57→	return &AuditPolicy{
    58→		LogCommands:   true,
    59→		LogNetwork:    false,
    60→		LogFilesystem: false,
    61→	}
    62→}
    63→

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//
     3→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     4→// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     5→// You may obtain a copy of the License at
     6→//
     7→//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package policy
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"regexp"
    19→	"strings"
    20→)
    21→
    22→// EnvironmentPolicy contains configuration for environment variable handling.
    23→type EnvironmentPolicy struct {
    24→	// ScrubSensitive enables automatic scrubbing of sensitive env vars.
    25→	ScrubSensitive bool
    26→
    27→	// AllowedVars is an allowlist of env var names to pass through.
    28→	// If empty, all non-sensitive vars are passed.
    29→	AllowedVars []string
    30→
    31→	// BlockedVars is a blocklist of env var names to always remove.
    32→	BlockedVars []string
    33→
    34→	// BlockedPatterns are regex patterns for env var names to block.
    35→	BlockedPatterns []string
    36→
    37→	// compiledPatterns caches compiled regex patterns.
    38→	compiledPatterns []*regexp.Regexp
    39→}
    40→
    41→// DefaultEnvironmentPolicy returns an EnvironmentPolicy with secure defaults.
    42→func DefaultEnvironmentPolicy() *EnvironmentPolicy {
    43→	return &EnvironmentPolicy{
    44→		ScrubSensitive: true,
    45→		BlockedVars: []string{
    46→			// Common secret/token vars
    47→			"GITHUB_TOKEN",
    48→			"GH_TOKEN",
    49→			"GITLAB_TOKEN",
    50→			"AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY",
    51→			"AWS_SESSION_TOKEN",
    52→			"AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET",
    53→			"GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS",
    54→			"OPENAI_API_KEY",
    55→			"ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",
    56→			"CLAUDE_API_KEY",
    57→			"NPM_TOKEN",
    58→			"DOCKER_PASSWORD",
    59→			"DOCKER_AUTH_CONFIG",
    60→			// Database credentials
    61→			"DATABASE_PASSWORD",
    62→			"DB_PASSWORD",
    63→			"MYSQL_PASSWORD",
    64→			"POSTGRES_PASSWORD",
    65→			"REDIS_PASSWORD",
    66→			"MONGO_PASSWORD",
    67→			// SSH/signing keys
    68→			"SSH_PRIVATE_KEY",
    69→			"GPG_PRIVATE_KEY",
    70→			"SIGNING_KEY",
    71→		},
    72→		BlockedPatterns: []string{
    73→			`(?i).*_SECRET.*`,
    74→			`(?i).*_TOKEN$`,
    75→			`(?i).*_API_KEY$`,
    76→			`(?i).*_PASSWORD$`,
    77→			`(?i).*_PRIVATE_KEY$`,
    78→			`(?i).*_CREDENTIALS$`,
    79→		},
    80→	}
    81→}
    82→
    83→// ScrubEnvironment filters environment variables according to the policy.
    84→// It takes env vars in KEY=value format and returns filtered vars.
    85→func (p *EnvironmentPolicy) ScrubEnvironment(env []string) []string {
    86→	if p == nil || !p.ScrubSensitive {
    87→		return env
    88→	}
    89→
    90→	// Compile patterns on first use
    91→	if p.compiledPatterns == nil && len(p.BlockedPatterns) > 0 {
    92→		p.compiledPatterns = make([]*regexp.Regexp, 0, len(p.BlockedPatterns))
    93→		for _, pattern := range p.BlockedPatterns {
    94→			if re, err := regexp.Compile(pattern); err == nil {
    95→				p.compiledPatterns = append(p.compiledPatterns, re)
    96→			}
    97→		}
    98→	}
    99→
   100→	// Build blocklist set for O(1) lookup
   101→	blocked := make(map[string]bool, len(p.BlockedVars))
   102→	for _, v := range p.BlockedVars {
   103→		blocked[v] = true
   104→	}
   105→
   106→	// Build allowlist set if specified
   107→	var allowed map[string]bool
   108→	if len(p.AllowedVars) > 0 {
   109→		allowed = make(map[string]bool, len(p.AllowedVars))
   110→		for _, v := range p.AllowedVars {
   111→			allowed[v] = true
   112→		}
   113→	}
   114→
   115→	result := make([]string, 0, len(env))
   116→	for _, e := range env {
   117→		parts := strings.SplitN(e, "=", 2)
   118→		if len(parts) != 2 {
   119→			continue
   120→		}
   121→		name := parts[0]
   122→
   123→		// Check allowlist (if specified)
   124→		if allowed != nil && !allowed[name] {
   125→			continue
   126→		}
   127→
   128→		// Check blocklist
   129→		if blocked[name] {
   130→			continue
   131→		}
   132→
   133→		// Check patterns
   134→		if p.matchesBlockedPattern(name) {
   135→			continue
   136→		}
   137→
   138→		result = append(result, e)
   139→	}
   140→
   141→	return result
   142→}
   143→
   144→// matchesBlockedPattern checks if a variable name matches any blocked pattern.
   145→func (p *EnvironmentPolicy) matchesBlockedPattern(name string) bool {
   146→	for _, re := range p.compiledPatterns {
   147→		if re.MatchString(name) {
   148→			return true
   149→		}
   150→	}
   151→	return false
   152→}
   153→

    16→
    17→// FilesystemPolicy defines filesystem access constraints for a sandbox.
    18→type FilesystemPolicy struct {
    19→	// EphemeralRoot when true means the root filesystem is ephemeral
    20→	// and changes are discarded after the sandbox is destroyed.
    21→	EphemeralRoot bool
    22→
    23→	// ReadOnlyPaths is a list of paths that should be mounted read-only.
    24→	ReadOnlyPaths []string
    25→
    26→	// HiddenPaths is a list of paths that should be hidden from the sandbox.
    27→	HiddenPaths []string
    28→
    29→	// WorkspaceMountPoint is where the workspace directory is mounted
    30→	// inside the sandbox. Default: /workspace
    31→	WorkspaceMountPoint string
    32→}
    33→
    34→// DefaultFilesystemPolicy returns a FilesystemPolicy with secure defaults.
    35→func DefaultFilesystemPolicy() *FilesystemPolicy {
    36→	return &FilesystemPolicy{
    37→		EphemeralRoot:       true,
    38→		ReadOnlyPaths:       []string{"/usr", "/bin", "/lib", "/lib64"},
    39→		HiddenPaths:         nil,
    40→		WorkspaceMountPoint: "/workspace",
    41→	}
    42→}
    43→
    44→// AddReadOnlyPath adds a path to the read-only list.
    45→func (p *FilesystemPolicy) AddReadOnlyPath(path string) {
    46→	p.ReadOnlyPaths = append(p.ReadOnlyPaths, path)
    47→}
    48→
    49→// AddHiddenPath adds a path to the hidden list.
    50→func (p *FilesystemPolicy) AddHiddenPath(path string) {
    51→	p.HiddenPaths = append(p.HiddenPaths, path)
    52→}
    53→

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//
     3→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     4→// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     5→// You may obtain a copy of the License at

     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package policy
    16→
    17→import "slices"
    18→
    19→// NetworkPolicy defines network access constraints for a sandbox.
    20→type NetworkPolicy struct {
    21→	// AllowedHosts is a list of hostnames that are allowed for egress.
    22→	// If empty, all egress is blocked by default.
    23→	AllowedHosts []string
    24→
    25→	// AllowedPorts is a list of ports that are allowed for egress.
    26→	// If empty, all ports are allowed for hosts in AllowedHosts.
    27→	AllowedPorts []int
    28→
    29→	// BlockAllEgress blocks all outbound network connections when true.
    30→	// This takes precedence over AllowedHosts.
    31→	BlockAllEgress bool
    32→}
    33→
    34→// DefaultNetworkPolicy returns a NetworkPolicy with secure defaults.
    35→// By default, all egress is blocked.
    36→func DefaultNetworkPolicy() *NetworkPolicy {
    37→	return &NetworkPolicy{
    38→		BlockAllEgress: true,
    39→		AllowedHosts:   nil,
    40→		AllowedPorts:   nil,
    41→	}
    42→}
    43→
    44→// AllowHost adds a host to the allowed list.
    45→func (p *NetworkPolicy) AllowHost(host string) {
    46→	p.AllowedHosts = append(p.AllowedHosts, host)
    47→}
    48→
    49→// AllowPort adds a port to the allowed list.
    50→func (p *NetworkPolicy) AllowPort(port int) {
    51→	p.AllowedPorts = append(p.AllowedPorts, port)
    52→}
    53→
    54→// IsHostAllowed returns true if the given host is allowed.
    55→func (p *NetworkPolicy) IsHostAllowed(host string) bool {
    56→	if p.BlockAllEgress {
    57→		return false
    58→	}
    59→	if len(p.AllowedHosts) == 0 {
    60→		return false
    61→	}
    62→	return slices.Contains(p.AllowedHosts, host)
    63→}
    64→

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//
     3→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     4→// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     5→// You may obtain a copy of the License at
     6→//
     7→//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→// Package sandboxtest provides test utilities for the sandbox package.
    16→package sandboxtest
    17→
    18→import (
    19→	"context"
    20→	"maps"
    21→	"sync"
    22→	"sync/atomic"
    23→	"syscall"
    24→
    25→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi"
    26→)
    27→
    28→// MockExecutor is a mock implementation of workspaceapi.Executor for testing.
    29→// It records all commands and signals for later inspection.
    30→type MockExecutor struct {
    31→	mu         sync.Mutex
    32→	pidCounter atomic.Int64
    33→	commands   map[workspaceapi.Pid]workspaceapi.Cmd
    34→	signals    map[workspaceapi.Pid][]syscall.Signal
    35→	closed     bool
    36→
    37→	// StartErr is the error to return from Start.
    38→	StartErr error
    39→
    40→	// SignalErr is the error to return from Signal.
    41→	SignalErr error
    42→
    43→	// CloseErr is the error to return from Close.
    44→	CloseErr error
    45→}
    46→
    47→// NewMockExecutor creates a new mock executor.
    48→func NewMockExecutor() *MockExecutor {
    49→	return &MockExecutor{
    50→		commands: make(map[workspaceapi.Pid]workspaceapi.Cmd),
    51→		signals:  make(map[workspaceapi.Pid][]syscall.Signal),
    52→	}
    53→}
    54→
    55→// Start records the command and returns a mock PID.
    56→func (m *MockExecutor) Start(
    57→	_ context.Context, cmd workspaceapi.Cmd,
    58→) (workspaceapi.Pid, error) {
    59→	if m.StartErr != nil {
    60→		return 0, m.StartErr
    61→	}
    62→
    63→	m.mu.Lock()
    64→	defer m.mu.Unlock()
    65→
    66→	pid := workspaceapi.Pid(m.pidCounter.Add(1))
    67→	m.commands[pid] = cmd
    68→
    69→	// Notify watcher immediately with no error
    70→	if cmd.Watcher != nil && cmd.Watcher.WatchProcess() != nil {
    71→		go func() {
    72→			cmd.Watcher.WatchProcess() <- nil
    73→		}()
    74→	}
    75→
    76→	return pid, nil
    77→}
    78→
    79→// Signal records the signal request.
    80→func (m *MockExecutor) Signal(pid workspaceapi.Pid, sig syscall.Signal) error {
    81→	if m.SignalErr != nil {
    82→		return m.SignalErr
    83→	}
    84→
    85→	m.mu.Lock()
    86→	defer m.mu.Unlock()
    87→
    88→	if _, ok := m.commands[pid]; !ok {
    89→		return syscall.ESRCH
    90→	}
    91→	m.signals[pid] = append(m.signals[pid], sig)
    92→	return nil
    93→}
    94→
    95→// Close marks the executor as closed.
    96→func (m *MockExecutor) Close() error {
    97→	m.mu.Lock()
    98→	defer m.mu.Unlock()
    99→
   100→	m.closed = true
   101→	return m.CloseErr
   102→}
   103→
   104→// Commands returns all commands that were started.
   105→func (m *MockExecutor) Commands() map[workspaceapi.Pid]workspaceapi.Cmd {
   106→	m.mu.Lock()
   107→	defer m.mu.Unlock()
   108→
   109→	result := make(map[workspaceapi.Pid]workspaceapi.Cmd, len(m.commands))
   110→	maps.Copy(result, m.commands)
   111→	return result
   112→}
   113→
   114→// Signals returns all signals sent to a process.
   115→func (m *MockExecutor) Signals(pid workspaceapi.Pid) []syscall.Signal {
   116→	m.mu.Lock()
   117→	defer m.mu.Unlock()
   118→
   119→	return append([]syscall.Signal{}, m.signals[pid]...)
   120→}
   121→
   122→// IsClosed returns whether the executor has been closed.
   123→func (m *MockExecutor) IsClosed() bool {
   124→	m.mu.Lock()
   125→	defer m.mu.Unlock()
   126→	return m.closed
   127→}
   128→

    14→
    15→package sandbox
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"fmt"
    20→	"net"
    21→	"path/filepath"
    22→	"sync/atomic"
    23→
    24→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/sandbox/sandboxrpc"
    25→	"google.golang.org/grpc"
    26→)
    27→
    28→// mockRuntime is a mock implementation of Runtime for testing.
    29→type mockRuntime struct {
    30→	vmCounter atomic.Int64
    31→	CreateErr error
    32→}
    33→
    34→func newMockRuntime() *mockRuntime {
    35→	return &mockRuntime{}
    36→}
    37→
    38→func (r *mockRuntime) Create(_ context.Context, config *Config) (VM, error) {
    39→	if r.CreateErr != nil {
    40→		return nil, r.CreateErr
    41→	}
    42→
    43→	id := fmt.Sprintf("mock-vm-%d", r.vmCounter.Add(1))
    44→	vm := &mockVM{
    45→		id:     id,
    46→		state:  VMStateRunning,
    47→		config: config,
    48→	}
    49→
    50→	if err := vm.startAgentServer(); err != nil {
    51→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("start agent server: %w", err)
    52→	}
    53→
    54→	return vm, nil
    55→}
    56→
    57→// mockVM is a mock implementation of VM for testing.
    58→type mockVM struct {
    59→	id         string
    60→	state      VMState
    61→	config     *Config
    62→	grpcServer *grpc.Server
    63→	listener   net.Listener
    64→	sockPath   string
    65→}
    66→
    67→func (v *mockVM) ID() string {
    68→	return v.id
    69→}
    70→
    71→func (v *mockVM) State() VMState {
    72→	return v.state
    73→}
    74→
    75→func (v *mockVM) AgentConn() (net.Conn, error) {
    76→	return net.Dial("unix", v.sockPath)
    77→}
    78→
    79→func (v *mockVM) Close() error {
    80→	v.state = VMStateStopped
    81→	if v.grpcServer != nil {
    82→		v.grpcServer.Stop()
    83→	}
    84→	if v.listener != nil {
    85→		_ = v.listener.Close()
    86→	}
    87→	return nil
    88→}
    89→
    90→func (v *mockVM) startAgentServer() error {
    91→	// Use /tmp directly to keep path short (macOS has 104 byte limit)
    92→	v.sockPath = filepath.Join("/tmp", fmt.Sprintf("sb-%s.sock", v.id))
    93→
    94→	lis, err := net.Listen("unix", v.sockPath)
    95→	if err != nil {
    96→		return fmt.Errorf("listen: %w", err)
    97→	}
    98→	v.listener = lis
    99→
   100→	v.grpcServer = grpc.NewServer()
   101→	sandboxrpc.RegisterAgentServer(v.grpcServer, sandboxrpc.NewServer())
   102→
   103→	go func() {
   104→		_ = v.grpcServer.Serve(lis)
   105→	}()
   106→
   107→	return nil
   108→}
   109→

    51→	config.WorkspaceRoot = t.TempDir()
    52→

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile a comprehensive report.

## Comprehensive Sandbox Package Analysis

Based on my thorough exploration of the sandbox package, here's a complete breakdown of the architecture, implementations, and current state:

### 1. RUNTIME INTERFACE DEFINITION

**Location:** `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox-direct/sandbox/runtime.go`

The core interface is minimal and elegant:

```go
// Runtime is the interface for creating and managing sandbox VMs.
type Runtime interface {
	// Create creates a new VM with the given configuration.
	// The VM is started and ready to accept connections.
	Create(ctx context.Context, config *Config) (VM, error)
}

// VM represents a running sandbox virtual machine.
type VM interface {
	// ID returns a unique identifier for this VM.
	ID() string

	// State returns the current state of the VM.
```

## Search Results Summary

I found comprehensive information about Apple's Containers framework for macOS 2025. Here are the key findings:

### Overview
Apple's Containerization is an open source project written in Swift to create and run Linux containers on your Mac. Container is a tool that you can use to create and run Linux containers as lightweight virtual machines on your Mac, and it's written in Swift, and optimized for Apple silicon.

### Architecture
Apple's Containerization framework implements a unique one-VM-per-container architecture where each Linux container operates within its own dedicated lightweight virtual machine, leveraging the macOS Virtualization framework for hardware-level isolation. Containerization does this by running each container inside of its own lightweight virtual machine while still providing sub-second start times.

### Technology Stack
Everything is written in Swift with no Go in sight. At the heart of each container VM runs vminitd, a custom init system written entirely in Swift.

### macOS Support
Container is supported on macOS 26, since it takes advantage of new features and enhancements to virtualization and networking in this release.

### Key Features
The framework provides APIs for image management, container execution, and a powerful init system built in Swift. The tool consumes and produces OCI-compatible container images, so you can pull and run images from any standard container registry.

content.content:
# Apple Container: CLI Tool Overview

Based on the GitHub repository page, here's what I found:

## 1. CLI Commands and Options

The page mentions basic commands like:
- `container system stop`
- `container system start`
- `/usr/local/bin/uninstall-container.sh -k` (keeps user data)
- `/usr/local/bin/uninstall-container.sh -d` (removes user data)

Full command details are referenced in the "[command reference](https://apple.github.io/container/documentation/)" but not detailed on this page.

## 2. Container Creation and Execution

The tool enables users to "create and run Linux containers as lightweight virtual machines on your Mac." It supports standard OCI-compatible container images, allowing you to pull from registries, run images, and push built images back to registries.

## 3. API/Library Interface

Container functionality is provided through the "[Containerization](https://github.com/apple/containerization)" Swift package for "low level container, image, and process management." The project offers "[API documentation](https://apple.github.io/container/documentation/)" separately.

## 4. System Requirements

- Mac with Apple silicon processor required
- macOS 26 minimum (new virtualization/networking features needed)
- No support for older macOS versions

## 5. Programmatic Usage

Limited information provided. The tool is written in Swift and can be consumed as a Swift package, though the page doesn't show code examples.

## 6. Networking, Filesystems, Resource Limits

Not detailed on this overview page. The "[technical overview](https://apple.github.io/container/documentation/)" document is referenced for deeper information.

content.content:
# Containerization Swift Package: API & Architecture Overview

## 1. Swift API for Creating and Running Containers

The package provides dedicated Swift types for container lifecycle management. According to the documentation, you can "[Spawn lightweight virtual machines and manage the runtime environment](https://apple.github.io/containerization/documentation/)" and "[Spawn and interact with containerized processes](https://apple.github.io/containerization/documentation/)."

Key capabilities include:
- **LinuxContainer.swift**: Manages VM lifecycle and runtime configuration
- **LinuxProcess.swift**: Handles process spawning and interaction within containers
- Object-oriented APIs abstracting virtualization details

## 2. VM Configuration APIs

The framework enables programmatic customization through:
- **Per-container kernel versions**: "Containerization provides first class APIs to use different kernel configurations and versions on a per container basis"
- **Networking setup**: "[Create] dedicated IP addresses for every container to remove the need for individual port forwarding"
- **Root filesystem management**: Access to ext4 filesystem creation and population APIs

## 3. Resource & Filesystem Interfaces

- **EXT4 filesystem APIs** (`ContainerizationEXT4`): Create and populate ext4 volumes
- **Netlink socket support** (`ContainerizationNetlink`): Low-level network configuration
- **OCI image management**: Pull, manipulate, and manage container images
- **Registry interaction**: Authenticate and interact with remote container registries

## 4. Low-Level C/Go Integration Points

The package includes:
- **C language components** (4.7% of codebase): Kernel-level operations
- **gRPC + Protobuf RPC**: vminitd provides "a GRPC API over vsock" for cross-VM communication
- Potential Go interoperability through gRPC interfaces and protobuf protocols

## 5. Architecture Summary

"Containerization executes each Linux container inside of its own lightweight virtual machine." The design prioritizes performance through an optimized Linux kernel (v6.14.9+) and minimal init system (vminitd), achieving "sub-second start times" while maintaining isolation via individual VMs rather than container sharing.

    39→	var missing []string
    40→
    41→	// e2fsprogs tools - required for ext4 image manipulation
    42→	e2fsTools := []string{"mke2fs", "debugfs", "resize2fs"}
    43→	for _, tool := range e2fsTools {
    44→		if _, err := exec.LookPath(tool); err != nil {
    45→			// On Linux, mkfs.ext4 is an alias for mke2fs
    46→			if tool == "mke2fs" {
    47→				if _, err := exec.LookPath("mkfs.ext4"); err != nil {
    48→					missing = append(missing, tool)
    49→				}
    50→			} else {
    51→				missing = append(missing, tool)
    52→			}
    53→		}
    54→	}
    55→
    56→	if len(missing) == 0 {
    57→		return nil
    58→	}
    59→
    60→	var instructions string
    61→	switch runtime.GOOS {
    62→	case "darwin":
    63→		instructions = `Install e2fsprogs using Homebrew:
    64→
    65→    brew install e2fsprogs
    66→
    67→After installation, ensure the tools are in your PATH. Homebrew may install
    68→them as keg-only, so you might need to add them to your PATH:
    69→
    70→    export PATH="/usr/local/opt/e2fsprogs/bin:$PATH"  # Intel Mac
    71→    export PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/e2fsprogs/bin:$PATH"  # Apple Silicon
    72→
    73→You can add this to your shell profile (~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc).`
    74→
    75→	case "linux":
    76→		instructions = `Install e2fsprogs using your package manager:
    77→
    78→    # Debian/Ubuntu
    79→    sudo apt-get install e2fsprogs
    80→
    81→    # Fedora/RHEL/CentOS
    82→    sudo dnf install e2fsprogs
    83→
    84→    # Arch Linux
    85→    sudo pacman -S e2fsprogs`
    86→
    87→	default:
    88→		instructions = "Install e2fsprogs for your operating system."
    89→	}
    90→
    91→	return &DependencyError{
    92→		Missing:      missing,
    93→		Instructions: instructions,
    94→	}
    95→}
    96→
    97→// checkE2fsprogs is a quick check for e2fsprogs availability.
    98→// Returns an error with installation instructions if not found.
    99→func checkE2fsprogs() error {
   100→	// Check for mke2fs (or mkfs.ext4 on Linux)
   101→	if _, err := exec.LookPath("mke2fs"); err != nil {
   102→		if runtime.GOOS == "linux" {
   103→			if _, err := exec.LookPath("mkfs.ext4"); err == nil {
   104→				return nil
   105→			}
   106→		}
   107→		return CheckDependencies()
   108→	}
   109→	return nil
   110→}
   111→

    15→package sandbox
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"fmt"
    20→	"io"
    21→	"net/http"
    22→	"os"
    23→	"path/filepath"
    24→	"runtime"
    25→)
    26→
    27→const (
    28→	// matchlockVersion is the version of Matchlock guest binaries to download.
    29→	// This should match the version in go.mod.
    30→	matchlockVersion = "v0.1.11"
    31→
    32→	// githubReleaseURL is the base URL for Matchlock GitHub releases.
    33→	githubReleaseURL = "https://github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock/releases/download"
    34→)
    35→
    36→// guestBinaryManager handles downloading and caching guest binaries.
    37→type guestBinaryManager struct {
    38→	cacheDir string
    39→	version  string
    40→}
    41→
    42→// newGuestBinaryManager creates a new guest binary manager.
    43→func newGuestBinaryManager() *guestBinaryManager {
    44→	home, _ := os.UserHomeDir()
    45→	return &guestBinaryManager{
    46→		cacheDir: filepath.Join(home, ".cache", "matchlock"),
    47→		version:  matchlockVersion,
    48→	}
    49→}
    50→
    51→// guestArch returns the architecture suffix for guest binaries.
    52→// Guest binaries are always Linux binaries (they run inside the VM).
    53→func guestArch() string {
    54→	// On macOS arm64, the VM runs Linux arm64
    55→	// On macOS x86_64, the VM runs Linux x86_64 (but we don't support this)
    56→	// On Linux, match the host architecture
    57→	if runtime.GOARCH == "arm64" {
    58→		return "linux-arm64"
    59→	}
    60→	return "linux-amd64"
    61→}
    62→
    63→// EnsureGuestBinaries downloads guest-agent and guest-fused if not present.
    64→// Returns the paths to the binaries.
    65→func (m *guestBinaryManager) EnsureGuestBinaries(ctx context.Context) (agentPath, fusedPath string, err error) {
    66→	arch := guestArch()
    67→
    68→	agentPath = filepath.Join(m.cacheDir, "guest-agent")
    69→	fusedPath = filepath.Join(m.cacheDir, "guest-fused")
    70→
    71→	// Check if binaries already exist and are valid
    72→	if m.isValidBinary(agentPath, arch) && m.isValidBinary(fusedPath, arch) {
    73→		return agentPath, fusedPath, nil
    74→	}
    75→
    76→	// Create cache directory
    77→	if err := os.MkdirAll(m.cacheDir, 0755); err != nil {
    78→		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("create cache dir: %w", err)
    79→	}
    80→
    81→	// Download guest-agent
    82→	agentURL := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s/guest-agent-%s", githubReleaseURL, m.version, arch)
    83→	if err := m.downloadBinary(ctx, agentURL, agentPath); err != nil {
    84→		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("download guest-agent: %w", err)
    85→	}
    86→
    87→	// Download guest-fused
    88→	fusedURL := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s/guest-fused-%s", githubReleaseURL, m.version, arch)
    89→	if err := m.downloadBinary(ctx, fusedURL, fusedPath); err != nil {
    90→		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("download guest-fused: %w", err)
    91→	}
    92→
    93→	return agentPath, fusedPath, nil
    94→}
    95→
    96→// isValidBinary checks if a binary exists and has the expected architecture.
    97→func (m *guestBinaryManager) isValidBinary(path, expectedArch string) bool {
    98→	fi, err := os.Stat(path)
    99→	if err != nil || fi.Size() == 0 {
   100→		return false
   101→	}
   102→
   103→	// Check ELF header for architecture
   104→	f, err := os.Open(path)
   105→	if err != nil {
   106→		return false
   107→	}
   108→	defer f.Close()
   109→
   110→	// Read ELF magic
   111→	magic := make([]byte, 4)
   112→	if _, err := f.Read(magic); err != nil {
   113→		return false
   114→	}
   115→	if magic[0] != 0x7f || magic[1] != 'E' || magic[2] != 'L' || magic[3] != 'F' {
   116→		return false
   117→	}
   118→
   119→	// Read e_machine at offset 18
   120→	if _, err := f.Seek(18, 0); err != nil {
   121→		return false
   122→	}
   123→	machineBytes := make([]byte, 2)
   124→	if _, err := f.Read(machineBytes); err != nil {
   125→		return false
   126→	}
   127→	machine := uint16(machineBytes[0]) | uint16(machineBytes[1])<<8
   128→
   129→	// EM_AARCH64=183, EM_X86_64=62
   130→	switch expectedArch {
   131→	case "linux-arm64":
   132→		return machine == 183
   133→	case "linux-amd64":
   134→		return machine == 62
   135→	}
   136→	return true
   137→}
   138→
   139→// downloadBinary downloads a file from URL to destPath.
   140→func (m *guestBinaryManager) downloadBinary(ctx context.Context, url, destPath string) error {
   141→	req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, url, nil)
   142→	if err != nil {
   143→		return fmt.Errorf("create request: %w", err)
   144→	}
   145→
   146→	resp, err := http.DefaultClient.Do(req)
   147→	if err != nil {
   148→		return fmt.Errorf("download: %w", err)
   149→	}
   150→	defer resp.Body.Close()
   151→
   152→	if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
   153→		return fmt.Errorf("download failed: HTTP %d", resp.StatusCode)
   154→	}
   155→
   156→	// Write to temp file first
   157→	tmpPath := destPath + ".tmp"
   158→	f, err := os.OpenFile(tmpPath, os.O_CREATE|os.O_WRONLY|os.O_TRUNC, 0755)
   159→	if err != nil {
   160→		return fmt.Errorf("create temp file: %w", err)
   161→	}
   162→
   163→	_, err = io.Copy(f, resp.Body)
   164→	if closeErr := f.Close(); closeErr != nil && err == nil {
   165→		err = closeErr
   166→	}
   167→	if err != nil {
   168→		os.Remove(tmpPath)
   169→		return fmt.Errorf("write file: %w", err)
   170→	}
   171→
   172→	// Atomic rename
   173→	if err := os.Rename(tmpPath, destPath); err != nil {
   174→		os.Remove(tmpPath)
   175→		return fmt.Errorf("rename: %w", err)
   176→	}
   177→
   178→	return nil
   179→}
   180→
   181→// ensureGuestBinaries is a convenience function to ensure guest binaries are downloaded.
   182→func ensureGuestBinaries(ctx context.Context) error {
   183→	mgr := newGuestBinaryManager()
   184→	_, _, err := mgr.EnsureGuestBinaries(ctx)
   185→	return err
   186→}
   187→

content.text:
Based on my research, I now have a clear understanding of the situation. Let me summarize what I found and present the options for a minimal Apple Containers-based implementation.

## Current State

The existing darwin runtime:
- Uses **Matchlock** (Go library wrapping Apple's Virtualization.framework)
- Requires **e2fsprogs** (`mke2fs`, `debugfs`, `resize2fs`) for ext4 image manipulation
- Downloads **guest binaries** (`guest-agent`, `guest-fused`) from GitHub
- Only works on **Apple Silicon (arm64)**

## Apple's Official Containerization (announced WWDC 2025)

Apple released two new projects:
- [apple/containerization](https://github.com/apple/containerization) - Swift framework for VM-based containers
- [apple/container](https://github.com/apple/container) - CLI tool built on top

Key characteristics:
- **One VM per container** - True hardware-level isolation
- **Written in Swift** - No Go bindings available
- **Requires macOS 26** - Uses new virtualization/networking features
- **gRPC over vsock** - vminitd provides process management inside VMs
- **No e2fsprogs needed** - Has built-in ext4 APIs
- **OCI-compatible** - Works with standard container images

content.content:
# vz: Go Virtualization Framework Binding

## Go API for Creating VMs

The vz library provides Go bindings to Apple's Virtualization.framework. Installation is straightforward:

```bash
$ go get github.com/Code-Hex/vz/v3
```

The package offers high-level APIs for VM creation and management, with examples available in the repository's example directory.

## Virtualization.framework Wrapper

vz wraps Apple's native framework, which "provides high-level APIs for creating and managing virtual machines (VM) on Apple silicon and Intel-based Mac computers."

Key capabilities include:
- Linux virtualization (x86_64, arm64)
- macOS virtualization on Apple Silicon (arm64)
- Support for VIRTIO device specifications

## Required Dependencies

- **OS**: macOS Big Sur (11.0.0) or higher
- **Go**: Latest version supports the last two major Go releases
- **Entitlements**: Binaries must have the `com.apple.security.virtualization` entitlement via code signing

For bridged networking, add the `com.apple.vm.networking` entitlement.

## Configuration Support

The library supports configuring:
- Memory and CPU allocation
- Disk storage
- Networking (including bridged attachments)
- Graphics and audio devices
- EFI boot loaders

## Running Commands Inside VMs

The documentation doesn't explicitly detail command execution APIs. The library focuses on VM lifecycle management and device configuration rather than guest-level command execution.

## Advanced I/O Features

**Supported**: 
- Virtio Sockets (vsock) for VM communication
- Serial console access
- Shared directories between host and guest

These features are documented in the repository's wiki with additional Linux kernel knowledge.

Excellent! Now I understand the Matchlock implementation better. The key components are:

1. **Code-Hex/vz** - Pure Go bindings to Apple's Virtualization.framework (no external dependencies needed!)
2. **vsock ports** - Used for communication with the guest (exec=5000, VFS=5001, ready=5002)
3. **Pre-built rootfs** - Can be passed via `config.PrebuiltRootfs` which skips the copy step
4. **NAT networking** - Simple networking without interception works out of the box

The user's questions:1. **Would Code-Hex/vz require external dependencies?** - NO! It's pure Go with cgo bindings to the macOS Virtualization.framework. No e2fsprogs needed.

2. **Could I embed an Alpine Linux image into the binary?**
   - YES! We can embed:
     - A pre-built Linux kernel (compiled for Virtualization.framework)
     - A minimal rootfs ext4 image
   - At runtime, extract to a temp file and pass to vz

The key challenge is creating the initial rootfs:
- We need a Linux kernel compiled for vz
- We need an Alpine rootfs as an ext4 image
- We need some minimal init that sets up the VM and accepts commands

Let me look at how Matchlock prepares its kernel/rootfs and what would be needed for a minimal implementation.

content.content:
1→# Matchlock
     2→
     3→> **Experimental:** This project is still in active development and subject to breaking changes.
     4→
     5→Matchlock is a CLI tool for running AI agents in ephemeral microVMs - with network allowlisting, secret injection via MITM proxy, and VM-level isolation. Your secrets never enter the VM.
     6→
     7→## Why Matchlock?
     8→
     9→AI agents need to run code, but giving them unrestricted access to your machine is a risk. Matchlock lets you hand an agent a full Linux environment that boots in under a second - isolated and disposable.
    10→
    11→When you pass `--allow-host` or `--secret`, Matchlock seals the network - only traffic to explicitly allowed hosts gets through, and everything else is blocked. When your agent calls an API the real credentials are injected in-flight by the host. The sandbox only ever sees a placeholder. Even if the agent is tricked into running something malicious your keys don't leak and there's nowhere for data to go. Inside the agent gets a full Linux environment to do whatever it needs. It can install packages and write files and make a mess. Outside your machine doesn't feel a thing. Every sandbox runs on its own copy-on-write filesystem that vanishes when you're done. Same CLI and same behaviour whether you're on a Linux server or a MacBook.
    12→
    13→## Quick Start
    14→
    15→### System Requirements
    16→
    17→- **Linux** with KVM support
    18→- **macOS** on Apple Silicon
    19→
    20→### Install
    21→
    22→```bash
    23→brew tap jingkaihe/essentials
    24→brew install matchlock
    25→```
    26→
    27→### Usage
    28→
    29→```bash
    30→# Basic
    31→matchlock run --image alpine:latest cat /etc/os-release
    32→matchlock run --image alpine:latest -it sh
    33→
    34→# Network allowlist
    35→matchlock run --image python:3.12-alpine \
    36→  --allow-host "api.openai.com" python agent.py
    37→
    38→# Secret injection (never enters the VM)
    39→export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-xxx
    40→matchlock run --image python:3.12-alpine \
    41→  --secret ANTHROPIC_API_KEY@api.anthropic.com python call_api.py
    42→
    43→# Long-lived sandboxes
    44→matchlock run --image alpine:latest --rm=false   # prints VM ID
    45→matchlock exec vm-abc12345 -it sh                # attach to it
    46→
    47→# Lifecycle
    48→matchlock list | kill | rm | prune
    49→
    50→# Build from Dockerfile (uses BuildKit-in-VM)
    51→matchlock build -f Dockerfile -t myapp:latest .
    52→
    53→# Pre-build rootfs from registry image (caches for faster startup)
    54→matchlock build alpine:latest
    55→
    56→# Image management
    57→matchlock image ls                                           # List all images
    58→matchlock image rm myapp:latest                              # Remove a local image
    59→docker save myapp:latest | matchlock image import myapp:latest  # Import from tarball
    60→```
    61→
    62→## SDK
    63→
    64→Matchlock also ships with Go and Python SDKs for embedding sandboxes directly in your application. Allows you to programmatically launch VMs, exec commands, stream output and write files.
    65→
    66→**Go**
    67→
    68→```go
    69→package main
    70→
    71→import (
    72→	"fmt"
    73→	"os"
    74→
    75→	"github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock/pkg/sdk"
    76→)
    77→
    78→func main() {
    79→	client, _ := sdk.NewClient(sdk.DefaultConfig())
    80→	defer client.Close()
    81→
    82→	sandbox := sdk.New("alpine:latest").
    83→		AllowHost("dl-cdn.alpinelinux.org", "api.anthropic.com").
    84→		AddSecret("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", os.Getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"), "api.anthropic.com")
    85→
    86→	client.Launch(sandbox)
    87→	client.Exec("apk add --no-cache curl")
    88→
    89→	// The VM only ever sees a placeholder - the real key never enters the sandbox
    90→	result, _ := client.Exec("echo $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")
    91→	fmt.Print(result.Stdout) // prints "SANDBOX_SECRET_a1b2c3d4..."
    92→
    93→	curlCmd := `curl -s --no-buffer https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages \
    94→  -H "content-type: application/json" \
    95→  -H "x-api-key: $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" \
    96→  -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01" \
    97→  -d '{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","max_tokens":1024,"stream":true,
    98→       "messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Explain TCP to me"}]}'`
    99→	client.ExecStream(curlCmd, os.Stdout, os.Stderr)
   100→}
   101→```
   102→
   103→**Python** ([PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/matchlock/))
   104→
   105→```bash
   106→pip install matchlock
   107→# or
   108→uv add matchlock
   109→```
   110→
   111→```python
   112→import os
   113→import sys
   114→
   115→from matchlock import Client, Config, Sandbox
   116→
   117→sandbox = (
   118→    Sandbox("alpine:latest")
   119→    .allow_host("dl-cdn.alpinelinux.org", "api.anthropic.com")
   120→    .add_secret(
   121→        "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"], "api.anthropic.com"
   122→    )
   123→)
   124→
   125→curl_cmd = """curl -s --no-buffer https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages \
   126→  -H "content-type: application/json" \
   127→  -H "x-api-key: $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" \
   128→  -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01" \
   129→  -d '{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","max_tokens":1024,"stream":true,
   130→       "messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Explain TCP/IP."}]}'"""
   131→
   132→with Client(Config()) as client:
   133→    client.launch(sandbox)
   134→    client.exec("apk add --no-cache curl")
   135→    client.exec_stream(curl_cmd, stdout=sys.stdout, stderr=sys.stderr)
   136→```
   137→
   138→See full examples in [`examples/go`](examples/go/main.go) and [`examples/python`](examples/python/main.py).
   139→
   140→## Architecture
   141→
   142→```mermaid
   143→graph LR
   144→    subgraph Host
   145→        CLI["Matchlock CLI"]
   146→        Policy["Policy Engine"]
   147→        Proxy["Transparent Proxy + TLS MITM"]
   148→        VFS["VFS Server"]
   149→
   150→        CLI --> Policy
   151→        CLI --> Proxy
   152→        Policy --> Proxy
   153→    end
   154→
   155→    subgraph VM["Micro-VM (Firecracker / Virtualization.framework)"]
   156→        Agent["Guest Agent"]
   157→        FUSE["/workspace (FUSE)"]
   158→        Image["Any OCI Image (Alpine, Ubuntu, etc.)"]
   159→
   160→        Agent --- Image
   161→        FUSE --- Image
   162→    end
   163→
   164→    Proxy -- "vsock :5000" --> Agent
   165→    VFS -- "vsock :5001" --> FUSE
   166→```
   167→
   168→### Network Modes
   169→
   170→| Platform | Mode | Mechanism |
   171→|----------|------|-----------|
   172→| Linux | Transparent proxy | nftables DNAT on ports 80/443 |
   173→| macOS | NAT (default) | Virtualization.framework built-in NAT |
   174→| macOS | Interception (with `--allow-host`/`--secret`) | gVisor userspace TCP/IP at L4 |
   175→
   176→## Docs
   177→
   178→See [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) for the full developer reference.
   179→
   180→## License
   181→
   182→MIT
   183→

content.content:
1→# Multi-stage Dockerfile for building Matchlock guest kernels.
     2→#
     3→# Stages:
     4→#   base            - Toolchain + kernel source (shared, heavily cached)
     5→#   build-x86_64    - x86_64 kernel compilation
     6→#   build-arm64     - arm64 kernel compilation
     7→#   output-x86_64   - Minimal output (scratch) containing only the kernel binary
     8→#   output-arm64    - Minimal output (scratch) containing only the kernel binary
     9→#
    10→# Usage:
    11→#   docker build --build-arg KERNEL_VERSION=6.1.137 \
    12→#     --target output-x86_64 --output type=local,dest=./out .
    13→
    14→ARG KERNEL_VERSION=6.1.137
    15→
    16→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    17→# Base: toolchain + kernel source
    18→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    19→FROM ubuntu:22.04 AS base
    20→
    21→ARG KERNEL_VERSION
    22→
    23→RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    24→        build-essential bc flex bison libelf-dev libssl-dev wget ca-certificates \
    25→        gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu \
    26→    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
    27→
    28→RUN wget -qO /tmp/linux.tar.xz \
    29→        "https://cdn.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v6.x/linux-${KERNEL_VERSION}.tar.xz" \
    30→    && mkdir /tmp/linux-src \
    31→    && tar xf /tmp/linux.tar.xz -C /tmp/linux-src --strip-components=1 \
    32→    && rm /tmp/linux.tar.xz
    33→
    34→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    35→# x86_64 kernel (Firecracker)
    36→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    37→FROM base AS build-x86_64
    38→
    39→COPY x86_64.config /tmp/linux-src/.config
    40→
    41→RUN cd /tmp/linux-src \
    42→    && make olddefconfig \
    43→    && make -j"$(nproc)" vmlinux
    44→
    45→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    46→# arm64 kernel (Virtualization.framework)
    47→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    48→FROM base AS build-arm64
    49→
    50→COPY arm64.config /tmp/linux-src/.config
    51→
    52→RUN cd /tmp/linux-src \
    53→    && make ARCH=arm64 CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-linux-gnu- olddefconfig \
    54→    && make ARCH=arm64 CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-linux-gnu- -j"$(nproc)" Image
    55→
    56→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    57→# Output stages (scratch = only the kernel binary, nothing else)
    58→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    59→FROM scratch AS output-x86_64
    60→COPY --from=build-x86_64 /tmp/linux-src/vmlinux /kernel
    61→
    62→FROM scratch AS output-arm64
    63→COPY --from=build-arm64 /tmp/linux-src/arch/arm64/boot/Image /kernel-arm64
    64→

content.content:
1→#!/bin/bash
     2→set -e
     3→
     4→# Build Linux kernels for Firecracker/Virtualization.framework
     5→# Uses a multi-stage Dockerfile for idempotent, layer-cached builds.
     6→# Requires Docker with BuildKit.
     7→
     8→KERNEL_VERSION="${KERNEL_VERSION:-6.1.137}"
     9→OUTPUT_DIR="${OUTPUT_DIR:-$HOME/.cache/matchlock/kernels/$KERNEL_VERSION}"
    10→OUTPUT_DIR=$(eval echo "$OUTPUT_DIR")
    11→ARCH="${ARCH:-all}"
    12→
    13→SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
    14→DOCKERFILE_DIR="$SCRIPT_DIR/../guest/kernel"
    15→
    16→print_usage() {
    17→    echo "Usage: $0 [OPTIONS]"
    18→    echo ""
    19→    echo "Options:"
    20→    echo "  KERNEL_VERSION=6.1.137   Linux kernel version (default: 6.1.137)"
    21→    echo "  OUTPUT_DIR=path          Output directory (default: ~/.cache/matchlock/kernels/\$VERSION)"
    22→    echo "  ARCH=x86_64|arm64|all    Architecture to build (default: all)"
    23→    echo ""
    24→    echo "Examples:"
    25→    echo "  $0                       # Build both architectures"
    26→    echo "  ARCH=arm64 $0            # Build only arm64"
    27→    echo "  KERNEL_VERSION=6.1.140 $0"
    28→}
    29→
    30→build_x86_64() {
    31→    echo "Building Linux kernel $KERNEL_VERSION for x86_64..."
    32→    DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build \
    33→        --build-arg KERNEL_VERSION="$KERNEL_VERSION" \
    34→        --target output-x86_64 \
    35→        --output "type=local,dest=$OUTPUT_DIR" \
    36→        -f "$DOCKERFILE_DIR/Dockerfile" \
    37→        "$DOCKERFILE_DIR"
    38→    echo "Built: $OUTPUT_DIR/kernel ($(du -h "$OUTPUT_DIR/kernel" | cut -f1))"
    39→}
    40→
    41→build_arm64() {
    42→    echo "Building Linux kernel $KERNEL_VERSION for arm64..."
    43→    DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build \
    44→        --build-arg KERNEL_VERSION="$KERNEL_VERSION" \
    45→        --target output-arm64 \
    46→        --output "type=local,dest=$OUTPUT_DIR" \
    47→        -f "$DOCKERFILE_DIR/Dockerfile" \
    48→        "$DOCKERFILE_DIR"
    49→    echo "Built: $OUTPUT_DIR/kernel-arm64 ($(du -h "$OUTPUT_DIR/kernel-arm64" | cut -f1))"
    50→}
    51→
    52→if [ "$1" = "-h" ] || [ "$1" = "--help" ]; then
    53→    print_usage
    54→    exit 0
    55→fi
    56→
    57→mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"
    58→
    59→echo "============================================"
    60→echo "Matchlock Kernel Builder"
    61→echo "============================================"
    62→echo "Version:    $KERNEL_VERSION"
    63→echo "Output:     $OUTPUT_DIR"
    64→echo "Arch:       $ARCH"
    65→echo "Dockerfile: $DOCKERFILE_DIR/Dockerfile"
    66→echo "============================================"
    67→
    68→case "$ARCH" in
    69→    x86_64|amd64)
    70→        build_x86_64
    71→        ;;
    72→    arm64|aarch64)
    73→        build_arm64
    74→        ;;
    75→    all)
    76→        build_x86_64
    77→        build_arm64
    78→        ;;
    79→    *)
    80→        echo "ERROR: Unsupported architecture: $ARCH"
    81→        echo "Supported: x86_64, arm64, all"
    82→        exit 1
    83→        ;;
    84→esac
    85→
    86→echo ""
    87→echo "Build complete!"
    88→echo "Kernels are in: $OUTPUT_DIR"
    89→ls -lh "$OUTPUT_DIR"
    90→

content.content:
1→package vsock
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"encoding/binary"
     5→	"fmt"
     6→	"net"
     7→	"os"
     8→	"syscall"
     9→	"unsafe"
    10→)
    11→
    12→const (
    13→	// AF_VSOCK is the address family for vsock
    14→	AF_VSOCK = 40
    15→
    16→	// VMADDR_CID_ANY accepts connections from any CID
    17→	VMADDR_CID_ANY = 0xFFFFFFFF
    18→	// VMADDR_CID_HYPERVISOR is the CID for the hypervisor (host from guest perspective)
    19→	VMADDR_CID_HYPERVISOR = 0
    20→	// VMADDR_CID_LOCAL is the CID for local communication
    21→	VMADDR_CID_LOCAL = 1
    22→	// VMADDR_CID_HOST is the CID for the host
    23→	VMADDR_CID_HOST = 2
    24→
    25→	// VMADDR_PORT_ANY accepts connections on any port
    26→	VMADDR_PORT_ANY = 0xFFFFFFFF
    27→
    28→	// IOCTL_VM_SOCKETS_GET_LOCAL_CID gets the local CID
    29→	IOCTL_VM_SOCKETS_GET_LOCAL_CID = 0x7B9
    30→)
    31→
    32→// sockaddrVM is the sockaddr_vm structure for vsock
    33→type sockaddrVM struct {
    34→	Family    uint16
    35→	Reserved1 uint16
    36→	Port      uint32
    37→	CID       uint32
    38→	Zero      [4]byte
    39→}
    40→
    41→// Addr represents a vsock address
    42→type Addr struct {
    43→	CID  uint32
    44→	Port uint32
    45→}
    46→
    47→func (a *Addr) Network() string { return "vsock" }
    48→func (a *Addr) String() string  { return fmt.Sprintf("%d:%d", a.CID, a.Port) }
    49→
    50→// Conn represents a vsock connection
    51→type Conn struct {
    52→	fd       int
    53→	local    *Addr
    54→	remote   *Addr
    55→	file     *os.File
    56→	isClosed bool
    57→}
    58→
    59→func (c *Conn) Read(b []byte) (int, error) {
    60→	return syscall.Read(c.fd, b)
    61→}
    62→
    63→func (c *Conn) Write(b []byte) (int, error) {
    64→	return syscall.Write(c.fd, b)
    65→}
    66→
    67→func (c *Conn) Close() error {
    68→	if c.isClosed {
    69→		return nil
    70→	}
    71→	c.isClosed = true
    72→	return syscall.Close(c.fd)
    73→}
    74→
    75→func (c *Conn) LocalAddr() net.Addr  { return c.local }
    76→func (c *Conn) RemoteAddr() net.Addr { return c.remote }
    77→
    78→func (c *Conn) SetDeadline(t interface{}) error      { return nil }
    79→func (c *Conn) SetReadDeadline(t interface{}) error  { return nil }
    80→func (c *Conn) SetWriteDeadline(t interface{}) error { return nil }
    81→
    82→func (c *Conn) File() *os.File {
    83→	if c.file == nil {
    84→		c.file = os.NewFile(uintptr(c.fd), fmt.Sprintf("vsock:%s", c.remote))
    85→	}
    86→	return c.file
    87→}
    88→
    89→// Listener represents a vsock listener
    90→type Listener struct {
    91→	fd   int
    92→	addr *Addr
    93→}
    94→
    95→// Listen creates a vsock listener on the given port
    96→func Listen(port uint32) (*Listener, error) {
    97→	return ListenCID(VMADDR_CID_ANY, port)
    98→}
    99→
   100→// ListenCID creates a vsock listener on the given CID and port
